author: John Chrysostom
book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles

title: Homily XXXVIII on Acts xvii. 16, 17.

source.title: NPNF1-11. Saint Chrysostom: Homilies on the Acts of the Apostles and the Epistle to the Romans

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Now while Paul waited for them at Athens, his spirit was stirred in him, when he saw the city wholly given to idolatry.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Therefore disputed he in the synagogue with the Jews, and with the devout persons, and in the market daily with them that met with him.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Observe how he meets with greater trials among the Jews than among the Gentiles.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Thus in Athens he undergoes nothing of this kind; the thing goes as far as ridicule, and there an end: and yet he did make some converts: whereas among the Jews he underwent many perils; so much greater was their hostility against him.—“His spirit,” it says, “was roused within him when he saw the city all full of idols.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Nowhere else were so many objects of worship to be seen.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But again “he disputed with the Jews in the synagogue, and in the market daily with them that met with him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then certain of the philosophers of the Stoics and Epicureans encountered him.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It is a wonder the philosophers did not laugh him to scorn, speaking in the way he did.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And some said, What does this babbler mean to say?” insolently, on the instant:—this is far from philosophy.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Other some said, He seemeth to be a setter forth of strange gods,” from the preaching, because he had no arrogance.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: They did not understand, nor comprehend the subjects he was speaking of—how should they? affirming as they did, some of them, that God is a body; others, that pleasure is the (true) happiness.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Of strange gods, because he preached unto them Jesus and the Resurrection:” for in fact they supposed “Anastasis” (the Resurrection) to be some deity, being accustomed to worship female divinities also.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And having taken him, they brought him to the Areopagus” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 19)—not to punish, but in order to learn—“to the Areopagus” where the trials for murder were held.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Thus observe, in hope of learning (they ask him), saying, “May we know what is this new doctrine spoken of by thee?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For thou bringest certain strange matters to our ears” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 20): everywhere novelty is the charge: “we would fain know therefore, what these things may mean.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It was a city of talkers, that city of theirs.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “For all the Athenians and strangers which were there spent their time in nothing else, but either to tell, or to hear some new thing.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then Paul stood in the midst of Mars hill, and said, Ye men of Athens, I look upon you as being in all things” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 21, 22)—he puts it by way of encomium: (the word) does not seem to mean anything offensive—δεισιδαιμονεστέρους, that is, εὐλαβεστέρους, “more religiously disposed.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For as I passed by, and beheld your devotions, I found an altar with his inscription, To an Unknown God.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What therefore ye ignorantly worship, this declare I unto you.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 23.)—“On which was inscribed, To an Unknown God.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The Athenians, namely, as on many occasions they had received gods from foreign parts also—for instance, the temple of Minerva, Pan, and others from different countries—being afraid that there might be some other god not yet known to them, but worshipped elsewhere, for more assurance, forsooth, erected an altar to that god also: and as the god was not known, it was inscribed, “To an Unknown God.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This God then, he tells them, is Christ; or rather, the God of all.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Him declare I unto you.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Observe how he shows that they had already received Him, and “it is nothing strange,” says he, “nothing new that I introduce to you.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: All along, this was what they had been saying: “What is this new doctrine spoken of by thee?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For thou bringest certain strange matters to our ears.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Immediately therefore he removes this surmise of theirs: and then says, “God that made the world and all things therein, He being Lord of heaven and earth”—for, that they may not imagine Him to be one of many, he presently sets them right on this point; adding, “dwelleth not in temples made with hands” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 24), “neither is worshipped with men’s hands, as though he needed anything”—do you observe how, little by little, he brings in the philosophy? how he ridicules the heathen error? “seeing it is He that giveth to all life, and breath, and all things; and hath made of one blood all nations of men for to dwell on all the face of the earth.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This is peculiar to God.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Look, then, whether these things may not be predicated of the Son also.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Being Lord,” he saith, “of heaven and earth”—which they accounted to be God’s.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Both the creation he declares to be His work, and mankind also.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Having determined,” he says, “the times assigned to them, and the bounds of their habitation,” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 25, 26), “that they should seek the Lord, if haply they might feel after Him, and find Him, though He be not far from every one of us: for in Him we live, and move, and have our being: as certain also of your own poets have said, For we are also His offspring.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This is said by Aratus the poet.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Observe how he draws his arguments from things done by themselves, and from sayings of their own.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Forasmuch then as we are the offspring of God, we ought not to think that the Godhead is like unto gold, or silver, or stone, graven by art.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And yet for this reason we ought.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: By no means: for surely we are not like (to such), nor are these souls of ours.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And imagination of man.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: How so? * * But some person might say, “We do not think this.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But it was to the many that he was addressing himself, not now to Philosophy.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: How then did they think so unworthily of Him?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Again, putting it upon their ignorance, he says, “Now the times of ignorance God overlooked.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Having agitated their minds by the fear, he then adds this: and yet he says, “but now he commandeth all men everywhere to repent.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Because He hath appointed a day, in the which He will judge the world in righteousness by that man whom He hath ordained; whereof He hath given assurance unto all men, in that He hath raised Him from the dead.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But let us look over again what has been said.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (Recapitulation.) (b) “And while Paul waited,” etc. (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It is providentially ordered that against his will he stays there, while waiting for those others. (a) “His spirit,” it says, “within him” παρωξύνετο.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It does not mean there anger or exasperation: just as elsewhere it says, “There was παροξυσμὸς between them.” (ch. xv.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 30.) (c) Then what is παρωξύνετο?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Was roused: for the gift is far removed from anger and exasperation.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He could not bear it, but pined away.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “He reasoned therefore in the synagogue,” etc. (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Observe him again reasoning with Jews.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: By “devout persons” he means the proselytes.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For the Jews were dispersed everywhere before (mod. text “since”) Christ’s coming, the Law indeed being henceforth, so to say, in process of dissolution, but at the same time (the dispersed Jews) teaching men religion.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But those prevailed nothing, save only that they got witnesses of their own calamities. (e) “And certain philosophers,” etc. (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: How came they to be willing to confer with him?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (They did it) when they saw others reasoning, and the man having repute (in the encounter).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And observe straightway with overbearing insolence, “some said, What would this babbler say?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For the natural man receiveth not the things of the Spirit.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Other some, He seemeth to be a setter-forth of strange deities: δαιμονίων, for so they called their gods.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And having taken him, they brought him,” etc. (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 19.) (a) The Athenians no longer enjoyed their own laws, but were become subject to the Romans. (g) (Then) why did they hale him to the Areopagus?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Meaning to overawe him—(the place) where they held the trials for bloodshed.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “May we know, what is this new doctrine spoken of by thee?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For thou bringest certain strange things to our ears; we would fain know therefore what these things mean.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For all the Athenians and strangers which were there spent their time in nothing else, but either to tell, or to hear some new thing.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Here the thing noted is, that though ever occupied only in this telling and hearing, yet they thought those things strange—things which they had never heard.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Then Paul standing in the midst of the Areopagus said, Ye men of Athens, I look upon you as being in all things more religiously disposed” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 22): (f) for the cities were full of gods (δαιμόνων, al. εἰδώλων): (h) this is why he says δεισιδαιμονεστέρους.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For as I passed by and viewed the objects of your worship —he does not say simply τοὺς δαίμονας (the demons, or deities), but paves the way for his discourse: “I beheld an altar,” etc. (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This is why he says, “I look upon you as being more religiously disposed,” viz. because of the altar.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “God,” he says, “that made the world.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He uttered one word, by which he has subverted all the (doctrines) of the philosophers.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For the Epicureans affirm all to be fortuitously formed and (by concourse) of atoms, the Stoics held it to be body and fire (ἐκπύρωσιν).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “The world and all that is therein.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do you mark the conciseness, and in conciseness, clearness?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Mark what were the things that were strange to them: that God made the world!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Things which now any of the most ordinary persons know, these the Athenians and the wise men of the Athenians knew not.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Seeing He is Lord of heaven and earth:” for if He made them, it is clear that He is Lord.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Observe what he affirms to be the note of Deity—creation.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Which attribute the Son also hath.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For the Prophets everywhere affirm this, that to create is God’s prerogative.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Not as those affirm that another is Maker but not Lord, assuming that matter is uncreated.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Here now he covertly affirms and establishes his own, while he overthrows their doctrine.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Dwelleth not in temples made with hands.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For He does indeed dwell in temples, yet not in such, but in man’s soul.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He overthrows the corporeal worship.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Did He not dwell in the temple at Jerusalem?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: No indeed: but He wrought therein.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Neither is worshipped by men’s hands.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: How then was He worshipped by men’s hands among the Jews?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Not by hands, but by the understanding.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “As though He needed anything:” since even those (acts of worship) He did not in this sort seek, “as having need.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Shall I eat,” saith He, “the flesh of bulls, or drink the blood of goats?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Neither is this enough—the having need of naught—which he has affirmed: for though this is Divine, yet a further attribute must be added.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Seeing it is He that giveth unto all, life and breath and all things.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Two proofs of Godhead: Himself to have need of naught, and to supply all things to all men.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Produce here Plato (and) all that he has philosophized about God, all that Epicurus has: and all is but trifling to this!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Giveth,” he says, “life and breath.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Lo, he makes Him the Creator of the soul also, not its begetter.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: See again how he overthrows the doctrine about matter.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And made,” he says, “of one blood every nation of men to dwell upon all the face of the earth.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: These things are better than the former: and what an impeachment both of the atoms and of matter, that (creation) is not partial (work), nor the soul of man either.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But this, which those say, is not to be Creator.—But by the mind and understanding He is worshipped.—“It is He that giveth,” etc. He not the partial (μερικοὶ δαίμονες) deities.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And all things.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It is “He,” he saith.—How man also came into being.—First he showed that “He dwelleth not,” etc., and then declared that He “is not worshipped as though He had need of aught.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: If God, He made all: but if He made not, He is not God.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Gods that made not heaven and earth, let them perish.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He introduces much greater doctrines, though as yet he does not mention the great doctrines; but he discoursed to them as unto children.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And these were much greater than those.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Creation, Lordship, the having need of naught, authorship of all good—these he has declared.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But how is He worshipped? say.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It is not yet the proper time.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What equal to this sublimity?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Marvellous is this also—of one, to have made so many: but also, having made, Himself sustains them (συγκρατεἵ) in being, “giving life and breath and all things. (b) And hath determined the times appointed, and the bounds of their habitation, that they should seek God, if haply they might feel after Him and find Him.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 27.) (a) It means either this, that He did not compel them to go about and seek God, but according to the bounds of their habitation: (c) or this, that He determined their seeking God, yet not determined this (to be done) continually, but (determined) certain appointed times (when they should do so): showing now, that not having sought they had found: for since, having sought, they had not found, he shows that God was now as manifest as though He were in the midst of them palpably (ψηλαφώμενος). (e) “Though He be not far,” he saith, “from every one of us,” but is near to all.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: See again the power (or, “what it is to be God,”) of God.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What saith he?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Not only He gave “life and breath and all things,” but, as the sum and substance of all, He brought us to the knowledge of Himself, by giving us these things by which we are able to find and to apprehend Him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But we did not wish to find Him, albeit close at hand.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Though He be not far from every one of us.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why look now, He is near to all, to every one all the world over!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What can be greater than this?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: See how he makes clear riddance of the parcel deities (τοὺς μερικούς)!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What say I, “afar off?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He is so near, that without Him we live not: “for in Him we live and move and have our being.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “In him;” to put it by way of corporeal similitude, even as it is impossible to be ignorant of the air which is diffused on every side around us, and is “not far from every one of us,” nay rather, which is in us. (d) For it was not so that there was a heaven in one place, in another none, nor yet (a heaven) at one time, at another none.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: So that both at every “time” and at every “bound” it was possible to find Him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He so ordered things, that neither by place nor by time were men hindered.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For of course even this, if nothing else, of itself was a help to them—that the heaven is in every place, that it stands in all time. (f) See how (he declares) His Providence, and His upholding power (συγκράτησιν); the existence of all things from Him, (from Him) their working (τὸ ἐνεργεἵν), (from Him their preservation) that they perish not.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And he does not say, “Through Him,” but, what was nearer than this, “In him.”—That poet said nothing equal to this, “For we are His offspring.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He, however, spake it of Jupiter, but Paul takes it of the Creator, not meaning the same being as he, God forbid! but meaning what is properly predicated of God: just as he spoke of the altar with reference to Him, not to the being whom they worshipped.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: As much as to say, “For certain things are said and done with reference to this (true God), but ye know not that they are with reference to Him.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For say, of whom would it be properly said, “To an Unknown God?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Of the Creator, or of the demon?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Manifestly of the Creator: because Him they knew not, but the other they knew.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Again, that all things are filled (with the presence)—of God? or of Jupiter—a wretch of a man, a detestable impostor!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But Paul said it not in the same sense as he, God forbid! but with quite a different meaning.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For he says we are God’s offspring, i.e. God’s own, His nearest neighbors as it were.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For lest, when he says, “Being the offspring of God” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 29), they should again say, Thou bringest certain strange things to our ears, he produces the poet.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He does not say, “Ye ought not to think the Godhead like to gold or silver,” ye accursed and execrable: but in more lowly sort he says, “We ought not.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For what (says he)?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: God is above this?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: No, he does not say this either: but for the present this—“We ought not to think the Godhead like unto such,” for nothing is so opposite to men.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “But we do not affirm the Godhead to be like unto this, for who would say that?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Mark how he has introduced the incorporeal (nature of God) when he said, “In Him,” etc., for the mind, when it surmises body, at the same time implies the notion of distance.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (Speaking) to the many he says, “We ought not to think the Godhead like unto gold, or silver, or stone, the shaping of art,” for if we are not like to those as regards the soul, much more God (is not like to such).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: So far, he withdraws them from the notion.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But neither is the Godhead, he would say, subjected to any other human conception.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For if that which art or thought has found—this is why he says it thus, “of art or imagination of man”—if that, then, which human art or thought has found, is God, then even in the stone (is) God’s essence.—How comes it then, if “in Him we live,” that we do not find Him?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The charge is twofold, both that they did not find Him, and that they found such as these.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The (human) understanding in itself is not at all to be relied upon.—But when he has agitated their soul by showing them to be without excuse, see what he says: “The times of ignorance God overlooked, but now commands all men everywhere to repent.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Are none of these men to be punished?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: None of them that are willing to repent.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He says it of these men, not of the departed, but of them whom He commands to repent.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He does not call you to account, he would say.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He does not say, Took no notice (παρεἵδεν); does not say, Permitted: but, Ye were ignorant.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Overlooked,” i.e. does not demand punishment as of men that deserve punishment.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Ye were ignorant.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And he does not say, Ye wilfully did evil; but this he showed by what he said above.—“All men everywhere to repent:” again he hints at the whole world.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Observe how he takes them off from the parcel deities!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Because He has appointed a day, in the which He will judge the world in righteousness by that Man whom He hath ordained, whereof He hath given assurance to all men, in that He raised Him from the dead.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Observe how he again declares the Passion.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Observe the terror again: for, that the judgment is true, is clear from the raising Him up: for it is alleged in proof of that.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: That all he has been saying is true, is clear from the fact that He rose again.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For He did give this “assurance to all men,” His rising from the dead: this (i.e. judgment), also is henceforth certain.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: These words were spoken indeed to the Athenians: but it were seasonable that one should say to us also, “that all men everywhere must repent, because he hath appointed a day, in the which He will judge the world.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: See how he brings Him in as Judge also: Him, both provident for the world, and merciful and forgiving and powerful and wise, and, in a word possessing all the attributes of a Creator.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Having given assurance to all men,” i.e. He has given proof in the rising (of Jesus) from the dead.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let us repent then: for we must assuredly be judged.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: If Christ rose not, we shall not be judged: but if he rose, we shall without doubt be judged.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “For to this end,” it is said, “did He also die, that he might be Lord both of the dead and living.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “For we shall all stand before the judgment seat of Christ, that every one may receive according to that he hath done.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 10, and 2 Cor. v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do not imagine that these are but words.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Lo! he introduced also the subject of the resurrection of all men; for in no other way can the world be judged.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And that, “In that He hath raised Him from the dead,” relates to the body: for that was dead, that had fallen.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Among the Greeks, as their notions of Creation, so likewise of the Judgment, are children’s fancies, ravings of drunken men.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But let us, who know these things accurately, do something that is to the purpose: let us be made friends unto God.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: How long shall we be at enmity with Him?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: How long shall we entertain dislike towards Him?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “God forbid!” you will say: “Why do you say such things?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: I would wish not to say the things I say, if ye did not do the things ye do: but as things are, what is the use now in keeping silence from words, when the plain evidence of deeds so cries aloud?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: How then, how shall we love Him?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: I have told you thousands of ways, thousands of times: but I will speak it also now.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: One way I seem to myself to have discovered, a very great and admirable way.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Namely, after acknowledging to Him our general obligations,—what none shall be able to express (I mean), what has been done for each of us in his own person, of these also let us bethink ourselves, because these are of great force: let each one of us reckon them up with himself, and make diligent search, and as it were in a book let him have the benefits of God written down; for instance, if at any time having fallen into dangers he has escaped the hands of his enemies; if ever having gone out on a journey at an untimely hour, he has escaped danger; if ever, having had an encounter with wicked men, he has got the better of them; or if ever, having fallen into sickness, he has recovered when all had given him over: for this avails much for attaching us to God.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For if that Mordecai, when the services done by him were brought to the king’s remembrance, found them to be so available, that he in return rose to that height of splendor (Esther vi.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 2–11): much more we, if we call to mind, and make diligent enquiry of these two points, what sins we have committed against God, and what good He has done to us, shall thus both be thankful, and give Him freely all that is ours.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But no one gives a thought to any of these things: but just as regarding our sins we say that we are sinners, while we do not enquire into them specifically, so with regard to God’s benefits (we say), that God has done us good, and do not specifically enquire, where, and in how great number and at what time.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But from this time forth let us be very exact in our reckoning.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For if any one can recall even those things which happened long ago, let him reckon up all accurately, as one who will find a great treasure.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This is also profitable to us in keeping us from despair.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For when we see that he has often protected us, we shall not despair, nor suppose that we are cast off: but we shall take it as a strong pledge of His care for us, when we bethink us how, though we have sinned, we are not punished, but even enjoy protection from Him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let me now tell you a case, which I heard from a certain person, in which was a child, and it happened on a time that he was in the country with his mother, being not yet fifteen years old.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Just then there came a bad air, in consequence of which a fever attacked them both, for in fact it was the autumn season.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It happened that the mother succeeded in getting into the town before (they could stop her); but the boy, when the physicians on the spot ordered him, with the fever burning within him, to gargle his throat, resisted, having forsooth his own wise view of the matter, and thinking he should be better able to quench the fire, if he took nothing whatever, therefore, in his unseasonable spirit of opposition, boy-like, he would take nothing.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But when he came into the town, his tongue was paralyzed, and he was for a long time speechless, so that he could pronounce nothing articulately; however, he could read indeed, and attended masters for a long time, but that was all, and there was nothing to mark his progress.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: So all his hopes (in life) were cut off, and his mother was full of grief: and though the physicians suggested many plans, and many others did so too, yet nobody was able to do him any good, until the merciful God loosed the string of his tongue (cf.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 35), and then he recovered, and was restored to his former readiness and distinctness of speech.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: His mother also related, that when a very little child, he had an affection in the nose, which they call a polypus: and then too the physicians had given him over and his father cursed him (for the father was then living), and (even) his mother prayed for him to die; and all was full of distress.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But he on a sudden having coughed, owing to the collection of mucus, by the force of the breath expelled the creature (τὸ θηρίον) from his nostrils, and all the danger was removed.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But this evil having been extinguished, an acrid and viscid running from the eyes formed such a thick gathering of the humors (τὰς λήμας), that it was like a skin drawn over the pupil, and what was worse, it threatened blindness, and everybody said this would be the issue.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But from this disease also was he quickly freed by the grace of God.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: So far what I have heard from others: now I will tell you what I myself know.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Once on a time a suspicion of tyrants was raised in our city—at that time I was but a youth—and all the soldiers being set to watch without the city as it chanced, they were making strict inquisition after books of sorcery and magic.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And the person who had written the book, had flung it unbound (ἀκατασκέυαστον) into the river, and was taken, and when asked for it, was not able to give it up, but was carried all around the city in bonds: when, however, the evidence being brought home to him, he had suffered punishment, just then it chanced that I, wishing to go to the Martyrs’ Church, was returning through the gardens by the riverside in company with another person.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He, seeing the book floating on the water at first thought it was a linen cloth, but when he got near, perceived it was a book, so he went down, and took it up.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: I however called shares in the booty, and laughed about it.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But let us see, says he, what in the world it is.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: So he turns back a part of the page, and finds the contents to be magic.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: At that very moment it chanced that a soldier came by: * * * then having taken from within, he went off.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: There were we congealed with fear.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For who would have believed our story that we had picked it up from the river, when all were at that time, even the unsuspected, under strict watch?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And we did not dare to cast it away, lest we should be seen, and there was a like danger to us in tearing it to pieces.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: God gave us means, and we cast it away, and at last we were free for that time from the extreme peril.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And I might mention numberless cases, if I had a mind to recount all.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And even these I have mentioned for your sakes, so that, if any have other cases, although not such as these, let him bear them in mind constantly: for example, if at any time a stone having been hurled, and being about to strike thee, has not struck thee, do thou bear this ever in thy mind: these things produce in us great affection towards God.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For if on remembering any men who have been the means of saving us, we are much mortified if we be not able to requite them, much more (should we feel thus) with regard to God.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This too is useful in other respects.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: When we wish not to be overmuch grieved, let us say: “If we have received good things at the hand of the Lord, shall not we endure evil things?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And when Paul told them from whence he had been delivered, (2 Tim. iv.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 17) the reason was that he might put them also in mind.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: See too how Jacob kept all these things in his mind: wherefore also he said: “The Angel which redeemed me from my youth up” (Gen. xlviii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 16); and not only that he redeemed him, but how and for what purpose.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: See accordingly how he also calls to mind the benefits he had received in particular.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “With my staff,” he says, “I passed over Jordan.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (Gen. xxxii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The Jews also always remembered the things which happened to their forefathers, turning over in their minds the things done in Egypt.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then much more let us, bearing in mind the special mercies which have happened to us also, how often we have fallen into dangers and calamities, and unless God had held his hand over us, should long ago have perished: I say, let us all, considering these things and recounting them day by day, return our united thanks all of us to God, and never cease to glorify Him, that so we may receive a large recompense for our thankfulness of heart, through the grace and compassion of His only begotten Son, with Whom to the Father, together with the Holy Ghost, be glory, might, honor, now and ever, world without end.
